title: Lesson 2: Basic Sentence Structure & the Verb 'To Be'   د جملې جوړښت او 'وي' کریا [cite: 16]
description: Unit 2 delivers your first full structural insight into Pashto. We will master the SOV (Subject-Object-Verb) word order, the copular verb 'to be' in all six persons, and navigate university campus vocabulary. [cite: 18]
duration: Textbook pp. 28–39 (approx. 10-15 pages of primary instruction plus Workbook exercises) [cite: 19]

content.heading: The Foundational Pivot: SOV Word Order
lines: Welcome to Unit 2! This lesson introduces the most critical cognitive shift in learning Pashto: understanding its sentence structure. [cite: 18]
Unlike English, which uses Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) order, Pashto is strictly an SOV (Subject-Object-Verb) or Subject-Complement-Verb language. [cite: 18]
Let's look at a 3-column contrast comparing English SVO with Pashto SCVerb structures: [cite: 18]
English: Farah is in Afghanistan. | Transliteration: farā pa afghānistān ke da. | Pashto: فرا په افغانستان کې ده. [cite: 14, 18]

content.text: Grammar Pro-Tip: In Pashto, yes/no questions have the exact same word order as statements! You distinguish a question purely by using rising intonation at the end of the sentence. This is a critical listening cue for English speakers accustomed to subject-auxiliary inversion. [cite: 18, 19]

content.heading: Campus Vocabulary & Prepositions
lines: Our communicative domain for this unit is the American university campus, keeping vocabulary immediately relevant to your daily life. [cite: 18]
Nouns: kor (کور) 'house', sinf (صنف) 'class', rughsṭiya (روغتون / روغستیا) 'hospital', leyliā (لیلیه) 'dormitory', shāgerd (شاګرد) 'student'. [cite: 18]
Adjectives: nāway (نوی) 'new', munṭazir (منتظر) 'waiting', nast (ناست) 'sitting'. [cite: 18]
Prepositions: pa … ke (په ... کې) 'in/at', la … sera (له ... سره) 'with', ta (ته) 'to/for'. [cite: 18]

Notice the locative prepositional phrase 'pa ... ke' wraps around the noun (e.g., pa sinf ke = in class). In fast speech, the 'pa' preposition often reduces to just 'p' before consonant-initial words! [cite: 18, 19]

content.heading: The Verb 'To Be' & Dropped Subjects
lines: Here is the full paradigm for the verb 'to be' in the present tense: [cite: 18]
1st Person: I am = zə yam (زه یم) / We are = mung yu (موږ یو). [cite: 18]
2nd Person: You (sg) are = tə ye (ته یې) / You (pl) are = tāso yəstay (تاسو یاست). [cite: 18]
3rd Person: He/She/It is = hagha da (هغه دی/ده) / They are = hagho di (هغوی دي). [cite: 18]
Note on Gender: The 3rd person singular 'da' can mean 'he is' or 'she is' depending entirely on context. [cite: 18]
Drop-Subject Rule: Pashto frequently omits pronouns when the subject is recoverable from the context or the verb ending! [cite: 18]

content.text: Negation Hack: To make a sentence negative, place the negative particle 'na' (نه) directly before the verb. For example, 'pa kor ke na da' means 'He/she is not at home'. [cite: 18]

content.heading: Script Practice: The Second Letter Family
lines: As we deepen script exposure, we integrate the second set of alphabet letters: the alef, wāw, ye diacritics, and the b/p/t/s shape-family. [cite: 18]
[Video Embed: Animated writing guide for the b/p/t/s shape family.]
[Interactive Writing Canvas]: Practice combining these letters to form basic campus vocabulary.
Remember our policy: Pashto script is required for all vocabulary items and example sentences, alongside romanised transliteration. [cite: 19]

content.heading: Cultural Context: Titles & Academic Settings
lines: When addressing male teachers, use the title 'sāyib' (صاحب - sir/Mr.) as a respectful sociolinguistic register marker. [cite: 18]
Example: Ghaldzay sāyib pa sinf ke da. (Ghaldzay sir is in class.) [cite: 18]
While mixed-gender academic settings are presented as normal for Afghan university contexts, always be aware that more conservative rural alternatives exist regarding gender interaction. [cite: 18]

content.question: How do you form a yes/no question in Pashto? [cite: 18]
options: By moving the verb to the beginning of the sentence.
By adding a question word at the start of the sentence.
By keeping the statement word order and using rising intonation.
By adding the particle 'na' before the verb.
content.explanation: In Pashto, questions have the identical word order to statements. They are distinguished purely by rising intonation at the end of the sentence. [cite: 18, 19]

content.question: Translate 'He/she is not at home' observing the strict Pashto SOV and negation rules. [cite: 18]
options: hagha da na pa kor ke
hagha na pa kor ke da
content.explanation: In Pashto, the negative particle [na] strictly precedes the verb [da], yielding [pa kor ke na da]. Thanks to the drop-subject rule, the pronoun can be omitted! [cite: 18]
